title: Module 4 quiz: Long context, RAG, and documents

questions.prompt: Your knowledge base is 12K tokens of stable docs. Build RAG or stuff?

choices.text: Stuff with `cache_control`. The corpus fits comfortably, caching makes repeat calls cheap, and you skip the multi-week build of embeddings + vector index + retrieval logic. Promote to RAG when the corpus is too big to stuff or when access control / multi-tenant requirements appear.

questions.explanation: Stuff small corpora, RAG large ones. The dividing line is roughly 30K tokens for cost reasons. Caching makes stuffing competitive on small data; build RAG when corpus size, query narrowness, or access scope makes retrieval necessary.

choices.text: Replaces vector embeddings with keyword search.

Generates a 1–3 sentence context per chunk (using Claude) and prepends it before embedding and BM25 indexing. The augmented chunk carries its original document context — entity names, dates, surrounding topic — so retrieval works on cross-referencing queries. Drops failure rate 35–67% depending on stack.

questions.explanation: Contextual Retrieval restores context that chunking destroys. Each chunk gets a short Claude-generated context prepended; both embeddings and BM25 are computed on the augmented version. Anthropic's writeup shows 35% reduction with embeddings alone, 49% with BM25 hybrid, 67% with reranking.

questions.prompt: Contextual Retrieval for a 1M-token corpus sounds expensive — running Claude on every chunk. What's the cost-control pattern?

choices.text: Skip Contextual Retrieval — it's not worth the cost.

Use Haiku + prompt caching. The whole document goes in the system block with `cache_control`; each chunk's context generation re-uses the cached doc at ~10% cost. Anthropic's writeup shows ~$1/M tokens of corpus — pennies to dollars one-time for the whole pipeline.
Generate context for only some chunks.

questions.explanation: Haiku + caching is the canonical pattern from Anthropic's research. The repeated whole-doc context is the expensive part; caching makes it nearly free on repeat calls. The chunk-specific output is small. Total cost is modest one-time, then forever free at query time.

questions.prompt: What's the gold-standard pattern for accuracy-critical answers (legal, medical, policy)?

choices.text: Built-in Citations API: pass documents with `citations: { enabled: true }`, surface citations in UI prominently. Combine with chain-of-thought (`<thinking>` + `<answer>`) so users see both the reasoning and the source text. Users can audit every claim in one click.

questions.explanation: Span-level citations + visible reasoning = auditable answers. Built-in citations point at exact text in the source; chain-of-thought shows how Claude got there. UI surfaces both so users can verify every claim — turning AI from black box to reading assistant.

questions.prompt: Receipts need to be turned into structured data. Old stack: OCR service + LLM + JSON parsing. New approach?

choices.text: Pass receipt images to Claude with a tool defining the receipt schema, and force tool_choice. Single call: Claude reads the image natively (OCR implicit) and returns typed structured data. One service replaces three; schema enforces validity.

questions.explanation: Vision + tool-call structured output is the standard modern pattern for document parsing. Cuts service count, reduces failures, makes the output schema-safe by construction. Used in production for receipts, invoices, IDs, forms, and many similar workflows.
